section.title: Overview
section.para: Stopwords are very common words (such as articles, prepositions, pronouns or conjunctions) that carry little semantic content of their own and are frequently removed prior to downstream processing such as indexing, classification, topic modeling, keyword extraction or similarity computation. Filtering stopwords can reduce noise, lower memory footprint and improve the signal-to-noise ratio of subsequent NLP components.

OpenNLP provides a small, dependency-free stopword filtering API in the opennlp.tools.stopword package. The central abstraction is the StopwordFilter interface, with a Dictionary-backed default implementation DictionaryStopwordFilter. Bundled stopword lists are available for eleven languages and can be loaded by ISO 639-1 code via the StopwordLists factory. Users may also load custom lists from any InputStream, mix them with the bundled defaults and add or remove individual entries at runtime.

OpenNLP further offers a tokenizer decorator (StopwordFilteringTokenizer) and an ObjectStream adapter (StopwordFilterStream) to plug stopword filtering into existing tokenization or training data pipelines, plus a command-line tool for ad-hoc filtering.

section.title: Loading a bundled list
Loading a custom list
section.para:
Custom stopword lists can be loaded from any InputStream via StopwordLists.load. The method takes the input stream, a character set and a flag indicating whether matching should be case-sensitive.
try (InputStream in = new FileInputStream("my-stopwords.txt")) { StopwordFilter filter = StopwordLists.load( in, StandardCharsets.UTF_8, false /* case-insensitive */); }
The expected file format is a plain UTF-8 text file with the following conventions:
One stopword entry per line.
Whitespace on a line separates the tokens of a multi-word entry, i.e. a line containing more than one token defines an n-gram stopword.
Blank lines are ignored.
Lines starting with # are treated as comments and ignored.
A small example file:
# my custom English stopwords the a an
# multi-word entries are defined by whitespace of the in spite of

section.title: Extending or overriding defaults
Multi-word stopwords
section.para:
Beyond single tokens, a StopwordFilter can match n-grams. The overloaded isStopword(String...) method accepts a sequence of tokens and returns true if the entire sequence is registered as a multi-word stopword entry.
StopwordFilter filter = DictionaryStopwordFilter.builder() .add("of", "the") .add("in", "spite", "of") .build();
filter.isStopword("of", "the"); // true filter.isStopword("in", "spite", "of"); // true filter.isStopword("of"); // false
Multi-word entries are matched as exact, contiguous sequences and are subject to the same case-sensitivity setting as the rest of the dictionary.

section.title: Decorating a Tokenizer
Streaming usage
Command-line usage
section.para:
Stopword filtering is also exposed as a CLI tool. The StopwordFilter command reads whitespace-separated tokens from standard input and writes the non-stopword tokens to standard output. The single argument is either the ISO 639-1 code of a bundled list, or a path to a custom stopword list file (same format as the Java API: one entry per line, with # comments and blank lines ignored, loaded case-insensitively).
$ echo "the quick brown fox" | opennlp StopwordFilter en quick brown fox
To use a custom list, pass its file path instead of a language code:
$ echo "the quick brown fox" | opennlp StopwordFilter my-stopwords.txt
A bundled language code takes precedence over a file of the same name; to force loading a file whose name happens to be a language code, qualify it with a path, e.g. ./en. The tool is intended for quick interactive checks and for use inside shell pipelines, for example chained behind a tokenizer:
$ opennlp SimpleTokenizer < article.txt | opennlp StopwordFilter en > filtered.txt

section.title: Bundled languages
section.para:
The following stopword lists are bundled with OpenNLP and can be loaded by their ISO 639-1 code through StopwordLists.forLanguage:
ISO 639-1 code Language
bgBulgarian
daDanish
deGerman
enEnglish
esSpanish
fiFinnish
frFrench
itItalian
nlDutch
ptPortuguese
ruRussian
The bundled lists are derived from the snowball stopword files shipped with Apache Lucene and retain their original BSD license; see the NOTICE and LICENSE files for the attribution and license text.

section.title: Thread-safety
section.para: DictionaryStopwordFilter is immutable once constructed and is therefore safe to share across threads without external synchronization. A filter returned by StopwordLists.forLanguage(...) or assembled via the Builder can be stored in a static field and accessed concurrently from any number of readers.

StopwordFilteringTokenizer and StopwordFilterStream are also stateless decorators with only final fields, so they inherit the thread-safety of the components they wrap. When paired with a DictionaryStopwordFilter and a thread-safe delegate tokenizer (e.g. SimpleTokenizer.INSTANCE or WhitespaceTokenizer.INSTANCE) the resulting pipeline is fully thread-safe.
